role_hints.role: Distribution field labor (door-to-door carriers, sample distributors, drivers)
role_hints.oews_basis: not in the listed top-10 — the record's role_mix is 4-digit 5418 (ad-agency-dominated) and completely hides the hand distributors, walkers and drivers (53-xxxx / 41-9011-adjacent) who are the actual workforce of circular/flyer/sample distribution firms; long tail, est. ~40-55% of a real 541870 wage bill
role_hints.note: Physically walking routes, hanging door pieces, stocking racks and driving delivery runs — no current AI substitutes. The record's high labor share (0.4285) is consistent with a labor-intensive distribution business, not an agency.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-9011 Demonstrators and Product Promoters 1.6% of wage bill in the 5418 proxy — but this is one of the few listed occupations that actually belongs to 541870-type work; understated for firms doing in-store demo/sampling
role_hints.note: In-person sampling and demo staffing is physical, scheduled gig labor; AI helps schedule it but cannot perform it.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3011 Advertising Sales Agents 8.2% + 41-3091 Sales Reps of Services 3.0% (5418 proxy, agency-weighted)
role_hints.note: Quoting routes/coverage, proposals and reporting automate; relationships with local advertisers and CPG/retail clients persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1161 Market Research Analysts 6.6% in the 5418 proxy; in real distribution firms this is a small routing/geo-targeting function, est. ~3-5%
role_hints.note: Route optimization, carrier-route targeting and coverage reporting are near-fully automatable — already largely software.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General & Operations Managers 9.3% (5418 proxy) + field supervisors in the unlisted tail
role_hints.note: Crew scheduling, verification (GPS proof-of-delivery) and payroll automate; managing a high-churn gig workforce stays human.

role_hints.oews_basis: long tail of office/admin occupations not in the listed top-10, est. remainder ~10-15%
role_hints.note: Billing, onboarding paperwork for gig distributors and client reporting automate near-fully.

source_hints.source: USPS Every Door Direct Mail (EDDM) pricing and Marketing Mail rate schedules
source_hints.why: USPS EDDM is the direct substitute that undercut private door-to-door distribution; its pricing sets the ceiling on what 541870 firms can charge.

source_hints.source: Valpak and Money Mailer franchise disclosure documents (FDDs)
source_hints.why: Shared-mail franchise FDDs are public and carry unit economics (fees, margins) for the largest branded players in ad-material distribution.
Tracks spend on print/insert/direct channels — the demand-melt evidence for the medium this industry distributes.

source_hints.source: Vericast (formerly Harland Clarke/Valassis) financial disclosures and credit-rating commentary
source_hints.why: The dominant shared-mail/insert player; its distress and restructuring history is direct evidence on the segment's economics and exit values.

source_hints.source: AFCP (Association of Free Community Papers) / local distribution association materials
source_hints.why: Small distribution firms cluster around free-paper and community-media networks; owner-demographic evidence may exist there — confirm the association still operates.

capture_questions: Distribution is sold per-piece / per-thousand against a USPS EDDM alternative whose price is public — when AI cuts the firm's routing, admin and sales costs, can it hold price at all, or does the USPS-anchored ceiling and client re-bidding pass every saving through as lower rates?

Who owns the client relationship — the distribution firm, or the retailers/CPG brands and agencies that treat coverage as a commodity bought on price and verified delivery? What switching cost exists beyond last-mile crew reliability?

The AI-bypass threat here is not generic AI doing distribution (it cannot walk routes) — it is advertisers using AI to shift the same budget to digital targeting entirely; what evidence shows local advertisers still buying physical distribution when AI-optimized digital alternatives are cheaper per response?

Verification is the trust product (did the pieces actually get delivered?) — does GPS/photo proof-of-delivery tech, now commoditized, erode the premium that organized firms charged over informal crews?
terminal_value_question: AI cannot hand a flyer on a doorknob — the operator's labor survives automation — but the product being distributed (printed advertising material) is in secular decline as AI makes digital substitutes cheaper and better-targeted; the mechanism that decides terminal value is advertiser ROI per dollar on physical circulars vs. AI-optimized digital, with USPS EDDM pricing as the floor competitor for whatever physical demand remains. Is this a cost-cut story on a structurally melting medium, and does the melt dominate?
special_notes: Role-mix contamination is severe: the 4-digit 5418 proxy shows ad-agency occupations (PR specialists, market research analysts) that essentially do not exist in flyer/sample distribution firms. The real wage bill is dominated by field distribution labor. Rebuild the V breakdown from the physical-labor structure; the 0.4285 labor share supports it.

Secular demand melt: print circular/insert distribution has been declining for 15+ years (retail insert cuts, newspaper collapse, Vericast/Valassis distress). Single-year data understates the trajectory — check volume trends, not just revenue snapshots, and let terminal-value reasoning dominate the score.

n_band oddity: even the 500+ employee class lands IN the $1-10M EBITDA band (13 firms, avg $58.8M revenue, implied $9.2M EBITDA) — there are no large-EBITDA firms in this code at the generic 15.65% margin. The real margin is likely thinner (labor-heavy distribution), which would shrink n_band (102) further. Treat the band count as fragile.

EBITDA normalization: distribution crews are heavily 1099/gig labor — worker-classification liability (misclassification suits) is a real diligence overhang that depresses multiples; owner comp sits inside SDE at the small end.

Boundary confusion: direct mail (the mailing itself) is 541860, shared-mail franchisors like Valpak straddle 541860/541870, in-store demo/merchandising firms overlap 541890 and 561990, and newspaper TMC (total market coverage) distribution sits inside publishers (511110). Any survey claiming to cover 'advertising distribution' needs its boundary checked.

The <5 employee class is 958 of 1,239 firms — mostly informal local crews. The organized roll-up universe is the 20-99 class (64 firms) plus a handful of regional players; N is small no matter how the margin shakes out.
